text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Yuen | DOB: on file | MRN: on file
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit Type: Urgent Follow-Up / Disease Reassessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IIIB (now Stage IV) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer (adenocarcinoma) presenting urgently for evaluation of new neurological symptoms and imaging-confirmed disease progression.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Yuen is a 64-year-old woman of Cantonese descent, former smoker (12 pack-years, quit 18 years prior to diagnosis), originally diagnosed approximately 14 months ago with Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, harboring an EGFR L858R mutation (exon 21, allele frequency 38.2% on initial biopsy), PD-L1 TPS 5%, ALK/ROS1-negative. Her disease was staged as T3 N3 M0 on initial PET-CT, with ipsilateral supraclavicular and contralateral mediastinal nodal involvement rendering her unresectable. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative for intracranial metastases. FoundationOne CDx NGS at diagnosis confirmed EGFR L858R as the sole actionable driver, with co-mutations in TP53 R248W, SMARCA4 R1192H (VUS), CDH1 E243K (VUS), low-level MET amplification (copy number ratio 2.4, not meeting threshold for high amplification), and CCND1 gain. TMB was 4 mutations/Mb (low) and MSS.

Given unresectable Stage III disease with a targetable EGFR mutation, the patient was started on Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily approximately 14 months ago as definitive first-line therapy; concurrent chemoradiation was offered and discussed at length but declined by the patient due to concerns about toxicity. Early toxicities included expected grade 1 acneiform rash, grade 1 paronychia, and grade 1 diarrhea, all managed conservatively without dose reduction.

She had an excellent initial radiographic response: restaging CT at 3 months showed the right upper lobe primary decreasing from 5.8 x 4.2 cm to 3.1 x 2.4 cm (approximately 47% decrease in longest diameter), with the right supraclavicular node no longer measurable and mediastinal nodes substantially smaller. At 6 months, the primary mass had decreased further to 2.0 x 1.6 cm, with mediastinal and hilar nodes normalizing. The 12-month CT showed the primary mass stable at 1.8 x 1.5 cm with no new lesions anywhere, and the concurrent brain MRI at that time was negative for intracranial metastases.

Approximately 3 weeks ago, Ms. Yuen began noting progressive bifrontal headaches, worse in the morning and partially responsive to acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours. Over the past 2 weeks, she has noticed word-finding difficulty -- she describes struggling to retrieve names of students and common vocabulary words -- which has been distressing to her given her profession as a high school math teacher. She denies focal weakness, sensory changes, visual disturbance, ataxia, or seizure activity. She has had no syncope. She continued Tagrisso through this period. She presented to our clinic this week after the symptoms persisted, and an urgent MRI brain with and without gadolinium was obtained.

MRI brain confirmed three new enhancing lesions: a 12 mm left frontal lesion with surrounding vasogenic edema (consistent with the dominant symptomatic lesion causing her word-finding difficulties given proximity to language cortex), an 8 mm right cerebellar hemisphere lesion without significant edema, and a 6 mm left parietal lesion. No leptomeningeal enhancement was identified. The left frontal lesion demonstrates mild mass effect without midline shift.

Concurrent CT chest, obtained at the same visit, demonstrated enlargement of the right upper lobe primary from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm in longest diameter, and a new right paratracheal lymph node measuring 1.1 cm -- indicating systemic progression in addition to CNS disease. There are no new hepatic, adrenal, or osseous lesions on the CT abdomen/pelvis.

A liquid biopsy (Guardant360, cell-free DNA) was sent at the time of progression suspicion and resulted today. Findings: EGFR T790M detected at allele frequency 0.6%; EGFR L858R detected at 1.2%; TP53 R248W at 1.1%. MET amplification is below threshold on this assay. EGFR C797S, PIK3CA, BRAF, and RET fusion are all negative. The detection of T790M is notable and somewhat unusual in the context of third-generation TKI (osi) therapy. osi was originally developed to overcome T790M resistance from first- and second-generation EGFR TKIs; however, rare reports of T790M re-emergence or pre-existing subclonal T790M during Tagrisso therapy have been described, potentially representing heterogeneous or polyclonal resistance. This will be discussed with our molecular tumor board.

In the interim, Tagrisso has been discontinued today given clear radiographic and clinical evidence of progression. The patient has been started on dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily to reduce vasogenic edema around the left frontal lesion. An urgent referral to radiation oncology has been placed for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) evaluation of all three intracranial metastases. The patient and her family (husband present today) have been counseled extensively about the diagnosis of disease progression.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Headaches (bifrontal, worse in the morning, 3-week history), word-finding difficulty (2-week history). Fatigue (mild, grade 1, ongoing from prior Tagrisso therapy). Mild residual facial papulopustular rash.

Negative: No focal weakness. No visual changes or diplopia. No seizures or loss of consciousness. No nausea or vomiting. No ataxia or gait instability. No hemoptysis (resolved with osi therapy initiation). No chest pain. No dyspnea at rest or exertion significantly beyond baseline. No cough appreciably worsened. No fever or chills. No bone pain. No abdominal pain or new GI symptoms. No lower extremity edema. No dysuria.

---

PAST ONCOLOGIC HISTORY
- Stage IIIB (now Stage IV) NSCLC, adenocarcinoma, right upper lobe, EGFR L858R mutant, diagnosed approximately 14 months ago
- Treated with osi 80 mg daily x 14 months (first-line, definitive intent)
- Best response: near-complete response in regional lymph nodes, partial response in primary tumor

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
- Non-small cell lung cancer (as above)
- No known coronary artery disease
- No known hypertension
- No known diabetes mellitus
- Former smoker

SOCIAL HISTORY
- High school math teacher; continues to work, though word-finding difficulties are impacting her professionally
- Immigrated from Hong Kong at age 32
- Married; husband is involved in her care and accompanied her today
- Former smoker, 12 pack-years, quit 18 years ago
- Occasional wine, 1-2 glasses per week
- Lives in a two-story home; manages activities of daily living independently

FAMILY HISTORY
- Paternal uncle: lung cancer (histology unknown)
- No other first-degree relatives with known malignancy

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS (at time of this visit)
- Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily -- DISCONTINUED today given progression
- Acetaminophen 1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (headaches)
- Topical clindamycin 1% solution (face, residual rash management)
- Urea-based moisturizer (daily)
- Multivitamin

New medication started today:
- Dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.1 degrees F, SpO2 96% on room air, weight 58.4 kg (down approximately 4 pounds from last visit 2 months ago), height 160 cm, BMI 22.8 kg/m2

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appears mildly anxious but cooperative. No acute respiratory distress.

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mild anomic aphasia evident on informal testing -- difficulty retrieving names of objects (e.g., pen, watch), intact sentence structure, no paraphasia. Follows complex commands without difficulty. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact bilaterally; visual fields full to confrontation; pupils equal, round, and reactive to light (3 mm bilaterally). No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. Facial symmetry intact. Upper and lower extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Finger-nose-finger coordination intact bilaterally. Gait steady, tandem gait performed without difficulty. Romberg negative. No pronator drift.

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No temporal tenderness. Oropharynx clear. Mild facial papulopustular rash, grade 1, predominantly on forehead and cheeks, consistent with EGFR TKI class effect.

Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable at this visit (previously palpable right supraclavicular node has been non-palpable for many months). No JVD.

Pulmonary: Breath sounds reduced at right apex compared to the left, unchanged from prior examinations. No wheezing, rales, or pleural rub. No dullness to percussion suggesting effusion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Normal bowel sounds.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Mild grade 1 paronychia involving right index finger, stable.

Skin: Grade 1 acneiform rash on face as noted. No other significant skin findings.

ECOG Performance Status: 1

---

LAB RESULTS (obtained today)

Complete Blood Count: WBC 6.8 x 10^9/L (normal differential), Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, normocytic, consistent with chronic disease), Platelets 241 x 10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min), BUN 14 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 84 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Albumin 3.7 g/dL, Glucose 102 mg/dL (note: will monitor on dexamethasone)
LDH: 198 U/L (mildly elevated, borderline)
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, PTT 28 seconds

Liquid Biopsy (Guardant360 ctDNA, resulted today):
- EGFR L858R: allele frequency 1.2% (detected)
- EGFR T790M: allele frequency 0.6% (detected -- unusual in context of osi third-generation TKI; see discussion below)
- TP53 R248W: allele frequency 1.1% (persistent clonal mutation)
- EGFR C797S: not detected
- MET amplification: below assay threshold
- PIK3CA: not detected
- BRAF V600E: not detected
- RET fusion: not detected

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today)

MRI Brain with and without gadolinium (obtained urgently this week):
Three new enhancing intracranial lesions identified:
1. Left frontal lobe, 12 mm, ring-enhancing, with surrounding vasogenic edema; mild mass effect on adjacent sulci, no midline shift. Location is near the inferior frontal gyrus / Broca's area territory, consistent with the patient's anomic aphasia symptoms.
2. Right cerebellar hemisphere, 8 mm, enhancing, without significant surrounding edema or mass effect.
3. Left parietal lobe, 6 mm, small enhancing focus, no edema.
No leptomeningeal enhancement. No hydrocephalus. No hemorrhagic transformation. Impression consistent with new intracranial metastases from known NSCLC primary.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (concurrent, obtained this week):
Right upper lobe mass has increased from 1.8 cm (12-month scan) to 2.7 cm in longest diameter. A new right paratracheal lymph node measures 1.1 cm short axis (previously normal). No new pulmonary nodules. No new mediastinal or hilar adenopathy beyond the above. No pleural effusion. No pericardial effusion. Abdomen: No hepatic lesions. No adrenal lesions. No new osseous lesions on osseous survey windows. Pelvis: Unremarkable.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

MS. MARGARET YUEN is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IV (previously III) EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma of the right upper lobe, presenting with imaging-confirmed systemic and intracranial disease progression following 14 months of osi (Tagrisso) monotherapy.

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION ON Tagrisso -- NEW INTRACRANIAL METASTASES AND SYSTEMIC PROGRESSION

The patient has developed clear-cut progression on osi evidenced by:
- Three new brain metastases on gadolinium-enhanced MRI (left frontal 12 mm with edema, right cerebellar 8 mm, left parietal 6 mm) -- this represents new M1b disease, upstaging to Stage IV
- Growth of right upper lobe primary from 1.8 cm to 2.7 cm
- New right paratracheal lymph node enlargement to 1.1 cm

The 14-month duration of response to osi monotherapy is broadly consistent with the median PFS seen in EGFR-mutant unresectable stage III/IV disease. The unusual finding of acquired EGFR T790M in the liquid biopsy in the context of osi resistance is noteworthy -- osi is a third-generation TKI specifically designed to inhibit T790M -- and likely reflects one of several possible scenarios: (a) a pre-existing minor subclonal T790M clone that was below detection at baseline and has expanded, (b) a newly acquired second-site T790M under different selective pressure than typical (unusual but documented), or (c) a heterogeneous resistance landscape in which T790M is co-present with other dominant resistance mechanisms. Additional tissue molecular profiling will be essential to characterize the full resistance landscape before determining the next systemic therapy approach.

IMPRESSION: Tagrisso resistance with multi-compartment progression. Staging revised to Stage IV (M1b, brain metastases).

PLAN:
- Tagrisso DISCONTINUED as of today.
- Dexamethasone 4 mg orally twice daily started immediately for management of vasogenic edema around left frontal lesion (and likely contributing to headaches and word-finding difficulty). Will monitor blood glucose given new steroid use; patient and husband educated on hyperglycemia symptoms and fingerstick monitoring.
- URGENT REFERRAL to Radiation Oncology for stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) evaluation of all three intracranial metastases. Given the small number and sizes of lesions (3 lesions, largest 12 mm), SRS is the preferred approach over whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) to minimize neurocognitive toxicity in this relatively young, high-functioning patient. Neurosurgical opinion is not indicated given the pattern and size of lesions.
- Tissue re-biopsy of the right upper lobe mass is being planned to fully characterize the resistance molecular landscape at progression -- this is critical for next-line therapy selection. We will coordinate with interventional radiology after SRS is complete.
- Molecular tumor board case submission: The unusual T790M detection on Tagrisso with concurrent MET amplification low-level signal (from original NGS) and the emerging resistance picture warrants multidisciplinary genomic review. Case submitted for next molecular tumor board.

2. CEREBRAL EDEMA / NEUROLOGICAL SYMPTOMS

Patient's morning-predominant headaches and anomic aphasia are most consistent with vasogenic edema surrounding the left frontal metastasis. Neurological exam reassuringly intact beyond the word-finding difficulty -- no motor deficits, no papilledema, no evidence of herniation.

PLAN:
- Dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily as above. Standard approach; will taper after SRS.
- Acetaminophen continued as needed for headache.
- Patient instructed to return to emergency department or call immediately for: new focal weakness, vision changes, seizure, worsening confusion, or rapidly worsening headache.
- Antiseizure prophylaxis: not routinely indicated for brain metastases; will defer to radiation oncology and neuro-oncology input.

3. SYSTEMIC THERAPY PLANNING (post-SRS)

Next-line therapy will depend heavily on tissue molecular profiling of the resistant tumor. Key considerations:
- The low-level T790M in the liquid biopsy is ambiguous as a therapeutic target in the Tagrisso-relapsed setting (C797S is not detected, but standard Tagrisso re-challenge is not appropriate without further data)
- Low-level MET amplification was present on baseline NGS (copy number ratio 2.4); in the context of osi resistance, MET amplification frequently rises to high-level and becomes a dominant resistance driver -- the tissue biopsy will be critical to assess this
- PIK3CA pathway activation and EGFR-independent bypass mechanisms should be evaluated
- Standard options being considered include: platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (carbo/peme given adenocarcinoma histology), clinical trial enrollment targeting specific resistance mechanisms identified on repeat tissue NGS
- Immunotherapy with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade has limited evidence in EGFR-mutant NSCLC and PD-L1 5% is borderline low; less favored as monotherapy in this context
- Patient has expressed strong interest in clinical trial participation; will research currently open trials for EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-osi progression, particularly those targeting MET-amplified, EGFR-mutant disease if tissue re-biopsy confirms high-level MET amplification

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND MONITORING

- Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 12.1 g/dL): will monitor; likely anemia of chronic disease. Iron studies to be checked at next visit.
- Mild LDH elevation: non-specific but will trend.
- Blood glucose monitoring initiated given new dexamethasone.
- Nutrition: Patient has had some weight loss over the disease course. Referral to oncology dietitian placed.
- Social work: Referral placed given disease progression, work impact (word-finding difficulties affecting teaching), and emotional burden on patient and family.
- Patient and husband received extensive counseling today regarding the diagnosis of disease progression, the plan for SRS, and the path forward. Both are engaged and asking thoughtful questions. Patient is emotionally distressed but determined.

CANCER STATUS: Present, progressing. Staging revised from IIIB to Stage IV (M1b, intracranial metastases).

RETURN VISIT: Patient will be seen in radiation oncology consultation within the next 5-7 days. She will follow up in our clinic within 2 weeks or sooner if symptoms change. Tissue re-biopsy planning will be coordinated during this interval.

Time spent: 65 minutes total, greater than 50% time spent on counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, Hematology/Oncology]
